coredata.dc:title: A colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode biosensor for point-of-care testing of β-lactoglobulin in milk based on DNAzyme and carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Zheng
Dang, Qiqi
Yang, Wenyi
Feng, Yufan
Sun, Chunyan
coredata.dc:description: β-Lactoglobulin (BLG) is a major allergen in milk and has proven to be a valuable biomarker for the assessment of milk quality. Herein, a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode biosensor was first constructed for detecting BLG based on the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs). The BLG aptamer was employed to generate G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, which exhibits peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity and serves as a specific recognition probe. The DNAzyme efficiently catalyzes the conversion of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into its blue oxidation product (oxTMB) and simultaneously quenches the fluorescence of O-CDs through the internal filter effect (IFE). Upon the presence of BLG, the aptamer specifically binds to BLG, thereby destroying the structure of the DNAzyme. Consequently, the POD-like activity of the DNAzyme is decreased, leading to an inhibition of oxTMB production and a restoration of the fluorescence of O-CDs. The BLG can be accurately measured by observing the changes in the colorimetric signal of oxTMB at 370 nm and the fluorescence signal of O-CDs at 580 nm. The developed biosensor demonstrates excellent linearity over a concentration range spanning from 1 μg·mL−1 to 500 μg·mL−1. Integrated with the smartphone platform, it could realize convenient quantitative analysis without instruments and meet the requirements of point-of-care testing (POCT). This study holds significant potential for ensuring food security.
coredata.dcterms:subject: β-Lactoglobulin
DNAzyme
Carbon dots
Dual-mode biosensor
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of dual-mode biosensor for BLG
Mechanism and feasibility of dual-mode biosensor
BLG detection in the real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of O-CDs. (B) Full XPS survey spectrum of O-CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of O-CDs. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of O-CDs under excitation at different wavelengths.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of oxTMB, fluorescence excitation spectra of O-CDs and fluorescence emission spectra of O-CDs. (B) The zeta potentials of O-CDs and oxTMB. (C) The fluorescence lifetime of O-CDs and O-CDs/oxTMB. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of O-CDs, TMB+, and O-CDs + TMB+. (E) Absorbance at 370 nm for O-CDs and TMB system. (F) FL Intensity at 580 nm for O-CDs and TMB system. Error bars indicate the standard deviations of three independent experiments.

(A) Fluorescence spectra with the different concentrations of BLG. Inset: the pictures of the corresponding solution. (B) Relationship between ΔF580 and the concentration of BLG. (C) Absorption spectra with the different concentrations of BLG. Inset: the pictures of the corresponding solution. (D) Relationship between ΔA370 and the concentration of BLG. The fluorescence intensity- responses (the red columns) (E) and absorbance responses (the blue columns) (F) of the biosensor. Error bars indicate the standard deviations of three independent experiments.

Screenshot of image color analysis software running on a smartphone: (A) HSB values of the analyzed image after processing. (B) Standard colorimetric cards are automatically generated by software. (C) The fitting curve between the BLG concentration and hue values. Error bars indicate the standard deviations of three independent experiments.
Schematic Diagram of the colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode biosensor for the detection of BLG based on G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and O-CDs.

head.ce:title: A colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode biosensor for point-of-care testing of β-lactoglobulin in milk based on DNAzyme and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, 130062, P R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Research Institute, Jilin University, Chongqing 401123, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Research Institute, Jilin University, Chongqing, 401123, P R China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130062, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: β-Lactoglobulin (BLG) is a major allergen in milk and has proven to be a valuable biomarker for the assessment of milk quality. Herein, a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode biosensor was first constructed for detecting BLG based on the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs). The BLG aptamer was employed to generate G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme, which exhibits peroxidase-like (POD-like) activity and serves as a specific recognition probe. The DNAzyme efficiently catalyzes the conversion of 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) into its blue oxidation product (oxTMB) and simultaneously quenches the fluorescence of O-CDs through the internal filter effect (IFE). Upon the presence of BLG, the aptamer specifically binds to BLG, thereby destroying the structure of the DNAzyme. Consequently, the POD-like activity of the DNAzyme is decreased, leading to an inhibition of oxTMB production and a restoration of the fluorescence of O-CDs. The BLG can be accurately measured by observing the changes in the colorimetric signal of oxTMB at 370 nm and the fluorescence signal of O-CDs at 580 nm. The developed biosensor demonstrates excellent linearity over a concentration range spanning from 1 μg·mL−1 to 500 μg·mL−1. Integrated with the smartphone platform, it could realize convenient quantitative analysis without instruments and meet the requirements of point-of-care testing (POCT). This study holds significant potential for ensuring food security.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food allergies are abnormal or overly strong immune responses caused by food proteins [1,2]. Cow milk protein (CMP), recognized as a major allergenic food by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), is responsible for roughly one-third of food allergy events [3]. BLG constitutes around 10 % of CMP, and it triggers 82 % of allergic reactions to CMP. When its mass concentration surpasses 0.1 mg·mL−1, allergic reactions can be induced, seriously endangering human health [4]. At present, there remains no efficacious treatment for food allergy. The sole approach is to scrupulously avoid foods containing allergens. Authorities have also advocated for the labeling of common food allergens, such as milk and other dairy products [5,6]. Consequently, it is imperative to develop effective methods for the detection of BLG. These methods can furnish food manufacturers with a foundation for allergen labeling and efficiently direct the daily dietary choices of allergic populations.

Traditional detection techniques for BLG, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [8], and other chromatographic methods, demand costly instruments and professional personnel for operation [9,10]. Moreover, they involve intricate sample pre-treatment procedures, rendering it arduous to achieve widespread dissemination and rapid on-site detection. In addition, antibodies, which are the core components of immunological methods, possess drawbacks such as large molecular weight, difficulties in modification, poor stability, and batch-to-batch variations [11,12]. Aptamers, serving as chemical antibodies, are single-stranded DNA or RNA molecules that possess the ability to bind to the target with high affinity [13]. Contrasted with traditional antibodies, aptamers are acquired via in vitro screening, obviating the need for animal and cellular experiments. They are inexpensive to prepare and are reproducible on a large scale. Additionally, aptamers have the advantages of facile modification, small molecular weight, good stability, and high affinity [14,15]. Since the aptamer of BLG was first screened by Eissa's group in 2016, single-mode biosensors based on BLG aptamers have garnered extensive attention [16]. G-quadruplex has been attracting much attention due to its rich conformation and designability, and G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme sensors have been widely used in food and environmental fields. However, due to bottlenecks such as low catalytic activity and unclear mechanism, the research progress and application expansion have been relatively slow in recent years [17,18]. Our group also reported a simple multicolor visualization sensing strategy by G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and TMB-mediated etching of gold nanorods [19]. The strategy has the advantages of excellent specificity, cost-effectiveness, and straightforward operation, and can be used for the POCT of BLG. Nevertheless, these methods rely on a single detection signal and are susceptible to the surrounding environment or human factors, leading to unreliable detection outcomes [20]. Dual-mode sensors with the ability to self-calibrate and improve signal reliability have received widespread attention in recent years [21,22]. Qian et al. designed a fluorescence and photoelectrochemical dual-mode biosensor using a programmable entropy driver circuit cascaded with a self-feedback DNA enzyme network. In addition to the advantages of high sensitivity and high specificity, the biosensor has good stability and reproducibility [23].

Inspired by the aforementioned research, a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode biosensor was developed to detect BLG based on the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and O-CDs ( Scheme 1). In the absence of BLG, the DNAzyme with POD-like activity catalyzes the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB. This reaction facilitates the effective occurrence of fluorescence quenching of O-CDs via IFE. Conversely, in the presence of BLG, BLG specifically binds to the aptamer and disrupts the structure of the DNAzyme. This disruption reduces the production of oxTMB and restores the fluorescence of O-CDs. Utilizing the BLG-induced changes in the colorimetric signal at 370 nm and the fluorometric signal at 580 nm, the constructed colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode biosensor was realized. Simultaneously, corresponding standard color cards were fabricated for the semi-quantitative detection of BLG. By capturing images using a smartphone, the image parameters were transformed into data information to enable the POCT of BLG. The developed sensing platform is anticipated to be a highly promising candidate method, offering the potential for practical applications in various scenarios related to BLG detection.

O-CDs were synthesized using a solvothermal approach. This synthesis was based on a previously reported method, yet with some modifications [24]. Typically, 120 mg of rhodamine B was subjected to sonication in 10 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, the reaction solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 160°C for 2 h. The supernatant was dialyzed within a dialysis bag for 12 h. Ultimately, the final O-CDs solution was successfully acquired.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of dual-mode biosensor for BLG
ce:section.ce:para: 20 μL of BLG aptamer (10 μM), 20 μL of hemin (10 μM), and 20 μL of different concentrations of BLG (0 ∼ 500 μg·mL−1) were incubated in 100 μL G4-incubation solutions (with 100 mM K+ and 100 mM NH4 +) at 37℃ for 20 min. Next, 40 μL of O-CDs (60 mg·mL−1) and 80 μL of TMB chromogenic solution (0.8 mM) were added in sequence. Then, the reaction solution was placed at 37°C for 10 min. For the control group, ultrapure water was used to replace BLG in an equal volume. Finally, the absorbance at 370 nm and fluorescence intensity at 580 nm were measured for the reaction system. BLG concentration was quantified by ΔA and ΔF, which represent the change in absorbance at 370 nm and the change in fluorescence intensity at 580 nm, respectively, between the experimental and control groups.

Moreover, the biosensor was placed in a ventilated enclosure illuminated by two LEDs, and a smartphone camera was used to take a picture of the sample at a fixed distance of 20 cm. The color recognition software installed on smartphones captured the image color and automatically converted the relevant parameters into HSB values. Subsequently, the hue (H) value was input into the fitting curve, thereby enabling the determination of the corresponding sample concentration.
The real sample analysis was recorded in the supplementary materials.

The synthesized O-CDs were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The TEM analysis unequivocally confirmed the successful preparation of O-CDs. These O-CDs showed excellent dispersion and a nearly spherical morphology, with an average diameter of 2.1 nm, as depicted in Fig. 1A. The full X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) demonstrated the presence of the elements C, N, and O (Fig. 1B). The coordination structure of the O-CDs was obtained by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. S1). The stretching vibrations at 3433 cm−1, 2974 cm−1, 1587 cm−1 and 1181 cm−1 in O-CDs, show the existence of O-H, C-H, CO and C-N bond, respectively. To explore the optical properties of O-CDs, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence emission spectroscopy were utilized (Fig. 1C). In the UV–vis spectrum of O-CDs, an absorption peak was clearly discernible at 557 nm. Notably, under different excitation wavelengths spanning from 325 to 405 nm, O-CDs demonstrated typical excitation-independent emission results (Fig. 1D). Specifically, when excited at 355 nm, O-CDs exhibited the maximum fluorescence emission intensity at 580 nm.

To evaluate the feasibility of O-CDs in the development of biosensors, photostability tests were carried out. Initially, the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs was observed to be predominantly stable across solutions with a wide range of salt concentrations, from low to high values (Fig. S2A). Subsequently, the fluorescence stability of O-CDs was examined in solutions with diverse pH levels. The results demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs remained stable within a pH range of 3.9–7.0 (Fig. S2B), indicating that they were stable in both acidic and neutral solutions. Moreover, O-CDs showed superior stability when stored at room temperature for 16 days (Fig. S2C). This finding emphasizes their potential for long-term stability in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism and feasibility of dual-mode biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the following experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 2A, the UV–vis absorption spectra of oxTMB showed a remarkable overlap with the fluorescence emission spectra of O-CDs. This observation strongly suggested a substantial probability of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE occurring between O-CDs and oxTMB [25]. As illustrated in Fig. 2B, the zeta potentials of O-CDs and oxTMB were measured to be +1.60 mV and +5.73 mV, respectively. Given these values, it is evident that oxTMB is unable to be adsorbed onto the surface of O-CDs via electrostatic interactions [26]. This finding suggests that FRET did not take place within this system. Moreover, the quenching mechanism was further verified by fluorescence lifetime tests. Fig. 2C reveals that the fluorescence lifetime of the O-CDs/oxTMB system remained nearly identical to that of O-CDs alone. This result indicates that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is based on the IFE [27]. Furthermore, by comparing the UV–vis absorption spectra of the O-CDs, 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine monovalent cation (TMB+), and O-CDs + TMB+ systems, no new absorption peaks were detected. This outcome demonstrates that O-CDs and the generated oxTMB merely underwent a simple mixing process and did not form a non-fluorescent complex. Consequently, the static quenching (SQE) mechanism can be excluded (Fig. 2D).

Building upon the aforementioned discoveries regarding the IFE process between oxTMB and O-CDs, the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and O-CDs system is capable of being effectively utilized for the colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode detection of BLG. The viability of this sensing system was also thoroughly explored. As depicted in Fig. 2E and Fig. 2F, it was clear that the aptamer and BLG alone were incapable of catalyzing the oxidation of TMB by H2O2, as indicated by the relatively high fluorescence intensity of the system. When hemin was present alone or in coexistence with BLG, there was no noticeable change in the absorbance of the system. However, a subtle decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed, suggesting that hemin possessed extremely feeble catalytic activity. The DNAzyme formed through the binding of the aptamer to hemin was capable of catalyzing the oxidation of TMB by H2O2, leading to the generation of a substantial amount of the blue oxidation product, the diimine derivative oxTMB. This oxTMB effectively quenched the fluorescence of the O-CDs. Nevertheless, the presence of BLG attenuated the catalytic activity of the DNAzyme. As a result, the oxTMB produced is reduced and the fluorescence intensity of O-CDs is restored to some extent. In light of these results, it can be concluded that the proposed colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode detection method for BLG is indeed feasible.

To improve the detection performance, several key conditions were optimized. The chromogenic environment within the TMB chromogenic solution is of utmost importance for detection [28]. When the pH was set at 3.3, the acidity of the chromogenic environment was overly high, impeding the catalytic reaction. The catalytic reaction efficiency reached its peak, and the signal output gradient was optimal when the pH of the chromogenic solution was 3.9. However, when the pH was continuously increased to 4.55, the signal output deteriorated, rendering it unfavorable for detection. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the fact that the isoelectric point of BLG is 5.2. As the pH value approaches its isoelectric point, the charge state of BLG undergoes alterations, potentially leading to structural changes and an increased aggregation tendency. Consequently, the optimal pH of the TMB chromogenic solution was determined to be 3.9 (Fig. S3A and S3B). Figs. S3C and S3D illustrated that the highest catalytic efficiency and the most favorable signal output gradient were achieved when the volume of H2O2 was 20 μL. An excessive amount of H2O2 was found to inhibit the catalytic reaction. Subsequently, the concentration of TMB and that of O-CDs were optimized. Figs. S3E and S3F demonstrated that as the concentration of the TMB chromogenic solution increased from 0.6 mM to 0.8 mM, the catalytic efficiency was enhanced, and the signal gradient became more pronounced. However, as the concentration of TMB continued to rise, the signal output of the BLG aptamer sensor deteriorated. This could be ascribed to the negative impact of excessive TMB on color development. Therefore, 0.8 mM was identified as the most appropriate addition concentration for TMB. Fig. S3G indicated that the best signal output was attained when the concentration of O-CDs was 60 mg·mL−1. Finally, the reaction temperature and time were optimized. The optimal reaction temperature and time were decided to be 10 min at 37°C (Fig. S2H and S2I).

Under optimal reaction conditions, various concentrations of BLG were measured to assess the sensing performance of the proposed biosensor. As depicted in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence signal of the G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and O-CDs system increased with the increasing BLG concentration. Specifically, the ΔF increased linearly in the concentration range of 1 ∼ 500 μg·mL−1. The linear relationship can be fitted with ΔF = 3.492 + 0.09 [BLG] (R2 = 0.997) (Fig. 3B). In accordance with the 3σ/slope rule, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.289 μg·mL−1. Conversely, as the BLG concentration rose, the colorimetric signal at 370 nm exhibited a gradual decline (Fig. 3 C). The ΔA increased linearly in the concentration range of 1 ∼ 500 μg·mL−1. The linear relationship can be fitted with ΔA = 0.0396 + 0.0013[BLG] (R2 = 0.994) (Fig. 3D). The LOD was determined to reach 0.413 μg·mL−1. Moreover, compared with single-mode BLG biosensors, the dual-mode BLG biosensor is anticipated to enhance the reliability and accuracy of the detection [29,30].

The specificity of the biosensor was appraised by employing milk proteins and allergens present in milk, including bovine serum albumin (BSA), casein, lactoferrin, and ovalbumin (OVA). Control experiments were carried out, with each protein at a concentration of 200 μg·mL−1. As depicted in Fig. 3E and Fig. 3F, the ΔF and ΔA values for BSA, casein, lactoferrin, and OVA at 200 μg·mL−1 were significantly lower than those of BLG at the same concentration (represented by the red and blue columns). Moreover, the corresponding concentrations of non-target substances, which were calculated from the linear regression equations, were significantly lower than those of BLG (as illustrated by the gray columns). This proves that the biosensor has good selectivity. In addition to this, it also has good anti-interference and reproducibility (Fig. S4 and Fig. S5). In comparison with other recently reported methods, the developed dual-mode biosensor demonstrated excellent detection performance (Table S1). Consequently, the colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode biosensors demonstrated outstanding anti-interference capabilities and excellent specificity in the detection of BLG [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: BLG detection in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applied potential of this biosensor in the real samples, recovery experiments were conducted on dairy products. Cow milk and camel milk powder samples were spiked with 100, 200, and 400 μg·mL−1 of BLG to investigate the impact of substrates on this biosensor. The spiked samples, with varying BLG concentrations, were then evaluated using both the colorimetric method and the HPLC method. As presented in Table S2, for the spiked cow milk samples, the recovery of this method varied from 94.99 % to 99.42 %, while for the spiked camel milk powder samples, the rates ranged from 94.90 % to 103.14 %. In both cases, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 6.0 %. The results of the two-tailed t-test analysis showed that there was no significant difference between the use of the developed dual-mode biosensor and the HPLC in the detection of BLG. The obtained results indicated that the dairy-product substrate exerted a negligible influence on the sensing performance of the developed BLG biosensor [33]. In summary, the established colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode biosensor for detecting BLG exhibits an acceptable anti-interference capacity and satisfactory reliability. This indicates that the method holds potential for practical application in the detection of dairy products.

In order to circumvent the utilization of costly large-scale instruments, POCT was further implemented to enhance the efficacy of this strategy [34]. The color of the solution was digitally captured by the built-in high-resolution camera of a smartphone. Subsequently, hue (H), saturation (S), and brightness (B) analysis of the output image was carried out using the color analysis software installed on the smartphone [35,36]. When taking points at various positions, even though there might be minor discrepancies in saturation and brightness, the acquired hue value remained largely unaffected ( Fig. 4A). This observation indicates that choosing the H channel for quantification can yield commendable stability, a crucial factor for practical detection. Based on the colorimetric card generated by the smartphone software, the concentration of BLG can be semi-quantitatively determined by the naked eye (Fig. 4B). Simultaneously, an image processing algorithm was employed. The H value of the images were designated as the vertical coordinate (y), while the BLG concentrations were assigned as the horizontal coordinate ((x), and a fitting curve was established (Fig. 4C) as follows: y = 0.089 x + 0.8691 (R 2 = 0.999). Consequently, the POCT assay developed in this study enables effortless semi-quantitative detection with the naked eye. Moreover, it can also provide accurate quantitative results through the utilization of a smartphone, demonstrating substantial advantages and promising application potential.

In summary, we fabricated a dual-mode biosensor based on G-quadruplex/hemin DNAzyme and O-CDs in the detection of BLG. The product of DNAzyme-catalyzed TMB, quenches the fluorescence of CDs through IFE. The aptamer possesses a specific recognition capability for BLG. In the presence of BLG, it binds to the aptamer, thereby impeding the formation of the DNAzyme. Consequently, the production of oxTMB within the system is diminished, and the fluorescence of O-CDs is restored. The biosensor demonstrates a broad detection range, extending from 1 μg·mL−1 to 500 μg·mL−1, with a remarkably low LOD of 0.289 μg·mL−1 and 0.413 μg·mL−1. It features excellent specificity, cost-effectiveness, and simplicity in operation, making it a promising tool for BLG detection. The colorimetric and fluorescence results mutually confirm each other, ensuring the accuracy of the sensing system. Additionally, a standard color card can be utilized for the semi-quantitative analysis of BLG concentration by the naked eye. Integrated with the smartphone platform, enabling accurate quantitative analysis and POCT. Consequently, the developed biosensor exhibits substantial potential for practical applications within the food safety domain.

Chunyan Sun: Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Wenyi Yang: Validation, Data curation. Yufan Feng: Visualization, Formal analysis. Zheng Liu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Qiqi Dang: Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20220101293JC) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0895).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2025.137976.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zheng Liu is a Ph.D. Degree Candidate under the supervision of Prof. Chunyan Sun at the Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University. His research topic is biosensors for detecting food harmful substances.
Qiqi Dang is a Master Degree Candidate under the supervision of Prof. Chunyan Sun at the Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University.
Wenyi Yang is a Bachelor Degree Candidate under the supervision of Prof. Chunyan Sun at the Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University.
Yufan Feng is a Bachelor Degree Candidate under the supervision of Prof. Chunyan Sun at the Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University.

Chunyan Sun is a Professor at College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University. She received her Ph.D. Degree from State Key Laboratory of Electrical Analysis, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry in 2006. Her research interest is food safety testing and control.
